The opening of Exodus presents a stark and disorienting shift. We begin with names—seventy souls who entered Egypt with Jacob—and we end with a decree of infanticide. The genealogy that opens the chapter reminds us that God had made Abraham a promise: his descendants would be as numerous as the stars. That promise had begun to unfold. Joseph had saved Egypt from famine, and his family had been welcomed. But then everyone died. Joseph died. His brothers died. The generation that had walked with him vanished. What remained was only the explosive multiplication of their children—a blessing that had become a threat in the eyes of a king who did not know Joseph's name or Joseph's God.

This is the logic of fear and power. A new Pharaoh looks at the growing Hebrew population and sees not a people but a problem. He does not ask who they are or where they came from. He does not remember that Joseph served Egypt faithfully. He calculates only what benefits him and what threatens him. So he moves to crush them. He multiplies their labor, darkens their days with brick and mortar, and finally—in an act of pure human wickedness—commands the slaughter of male children. The cruelty is methodical. It is written into law. It is practical. And it reveals how desperately human power fears what it cannot control.

Yet something extraordinary happens in the midst of this darkness. Two midwives—Shiphrah and Puah—choose to fear God more than Pharaoh. This is not the grand heroism of armies or kings. It is the quiet, dangerous courage of women who attend births in the darkness, who hear the cries of mothers, and who refuse to become instruments of murder. They do not lead a rebellion or deliver speeches. They simply let the boys live. When confronted, they offer a reason that preserves both their lives and their integrity. And God rewards their fear of Him with families of their own. The Pharaoh commands death. God commands life. The midwives choose to align themselves with life, with God, and with the future He alone controls.

Exodus 1 shows us that God's promises do not fail, even when they seem to be drowning in cruelty and darkness. The very thing Pharaoh fears—the multiplication of the Hebrews—accelerates under oppression. The more he crushes them, the more they grow. His power, which seemed absolute, cannot stop the tide of life that God has set in motion. And in the smallest acts of faith—two women choosing obedience over survival—the character of God's kingdom is revealed. We are not called to match the world's power or to play its games. We are called to fear God, to choose life, and to trust that His purposes will not be thwarted by any throne on earth.
